title: The data center backlash isn't just NIMBYism
gist: A wide-ranging mailbag arguing that data-center opposition reflects genuine public skepticism of AI's societal impact (not classic NIMBYism), that Democrats should copy Graham Platner's rhetorical moderation rather than his demographics, that Georgia's governor race is the single most important 2026 state race, and that both nuclear and solar buildout are hamstrung more by permitting and 'energy fandom' than by fossil-fuel lobbying.

insights.claim: Nate Cohn's polling found fewer Maine voters saw Graham Platner as 'too far to the left' than saw the Democratic Party overall as too left-wing, even though Platner held views to the left of public opinion on many issues, including trans issues, which are common across almost all Democrats.
insights.speaker: Matthew Yglesias
insights.so_what: Perception of ideological extremity is decoupled from actual policy positions — a data point for assessing which Democratic candidates can outperform the party's brand.
insights.quote: Fewer Maine voters thought he was 'too far to the left' than said the same of the Democratic Party in general.
insights.locator: Nate Cohn did an interesting piece

insights.claim: What made Platner read as moderate was not his demographics but specific rhetorical and policy choices: forceful opposition to assault weapons bans (putting him on the party's rightward edge on guns) and avoidance of progressive linguistic markers like land acknowledgments, 'birthing people,' BIPOC, or Latinx.
insights.speaker: Matthew Yglesias
insights.so_what: Actionable playbook for Democratic candidates seeking a moderate image: adopt select substantive moderate positions (e.g., guns) and drop campus-activist linguistic cues, rather than relying on identity alone.
insights.locator: if you want to be perceived as more moderate

insights.claim: The data-center backlash is broader than ordinary NIMBYism because political momentum for statewide bans is emerging even in places far from any proposed site, unlike typical local fights over apartments, solar panels, or batteries.
insights.speaker: Matthew Yglesias
insights.so_what: Signals regulatory risk for data-center/AI infrastructure developers extends beyond local siting politics into state-level legislative risk.
insights.locator: What we're seeing in terms of political momentum for statewide bans

insights.claim: The economically coherent policy response to data-center electricity-price concerns is to tighten permit terms so projects must be net-beneficial to ratepayers, not to ban data centers outright.
insights.speaker: Matthew Yglesias
insights.so_what: A concrete regulatory design investors in power/data-center infrastructure should watch for: ratepayer-benefit conditions attached to permits rather than blanket moratoria.
insights.quote: you can build the data center only if you agree to terms that make it net beneficial to ratepayers

insights.claim: Polling cited from Blue Rose Research/The Bully Pulpit shows the public is moderately skeptical of AI's impact on their own lives and more skeptical of its impact on society, is unpersuaded by 'race with China' arguments, and is not enthusiastic about utopian post-work visions.
insights.speaker: Matthew Yglesias
insights.so_what: Direct polling evidence AI companies and policymakers should weigh when crafting public messaging or anticipating backlash risk to AI infrastructure buildout.
insights.locator: Source: The Bully Pulpit/Blue Rose Research

insights.claim: In response to skeptical polling, AI companies have shifted messaging away from utopian rhetoric toward reassuring claims that their models will be productivity-enhancing without disrupting labor markets — a claim Yglesias views as potentially incoherent given that technological change over the past 200 years, while not eliminating labor overall, has repeatedly eliminated specific jobs and whole fields.
insights.speaker: Matthew Yglesias
insights.so_what: Flags a messaging vulnerability for AI companies: 'no labor disruption' claims may not hold up and could become a political liability as job losses in specific fields materialize.
insights.locator: A.I. companies have mostly pivoted away from the utopian rhetoric

insights.claim: Restricting where data centers can be physically built is a weak lever for AI policy overall, since a country's population can be economically disrupted by AI progress even if no data center is ever built within its borders; effective AI policy requires separate rules on economic consequences and AI governance.
insights.speaker: Matthew Yglesias
insights.so_what: Argues siting bans are largely symbolic/ineffective policy tools — investors should expect the more consequential AI regulation to come through labor/economic and governance rules, not zoning fights.
insights.locator: altering the physical location of data centers is actually a very weak lever

insights.claim: Many contemporary Westerners hold an incoherent combination of nostalgia for a past era of rapid physical/landscape change while opposing new construction today, creating a feedback loop where opposition to building produces stagnation, which in turn deepens the nostalgia.
insights.speaker: Matthew Yglesias
insights.so_what: A general mental model for why anti-development sentiment persists even among people who claim to want dynamism — relevant across housing, energy, and infrastructure investment theses.
insights.locator: A lot of contemporary Westerners have a basically incoherent view

insights.claim: Overcoming NIMBY-style skepticism toward new construction requires a baseline public belief that the thing being built is actually good — a belief that broadly exists for housing but is much shakier for AI/data centers, which is why housing NIMBYism and data-center opposition differ in kind.
insights.speaker: Matthew Yglesias
insights.so_what: Explains why standard pro-housing YIMBY tactics may not transfer directly to building public support for AI infrastructure.
insights.locator: Doing that, though, requires some kind of baseline view

insights.claim: Colorado's outsized representation of moderate, Slow Boring-aligned elected officials (Jared Polis, John Hickenlooper, Michael Bennet, Joe Neguse, Jason Crow, Brittany Pettersen) is attributed to the state's recent shift from red to blue: in 2004 Colorado was redder than Nevada, Ohio, or Iowa, so its current officeholders mostly won genuinely competitive races rather than emerging from safe machine politics, unlike a state such as New York.
insights.speaker: Matthew Yglesias
insights.so_what: A model for predicting which newly-competitive states will produce pragmatic vs. ideological political classes: recent electoral competitiveness correlates with more moderate, capable officeholders.
insights.locator: Back in 2004, Colorado was redder than Nevada, Ohio, or Iowa

themes.theme: Mamdani, Trump, and NYC fiscal reality

insights.claim: Trump and Mamdani share a structural political dynamic, not an ideological one: both maintain maximalist rhetoric while moderating in practice, and both have a base of supporters who reinterpret any compromise or cordial meeting with an opponent as a symbol of their leader's mastery rather than a concession — a personalistic-following pattern that also applied to Obama, Clinton, and Bush.
insights.speaker: Matthew Yglesias
insights.so_what: A reusable framework for interpreting how populist figures across the spectrum manage the gap between rhetoric and governance without losing their base.
insights.locator: What I think is "Trumpian" about Mamdani

insights.claim: New York City has genuine underlying economic capacity to raise tax revenue (the pied-a-terre tax is 'clever'), but too much of that revenue is captured by entrenched interest groups ('stationary bandits') rather than converted into services — a structural problem neither Bill de Blasio nor Michael Bloomberg solved, and one Mamdani's policies (largely continuous with de Blasio's) are now running into.
insights.speaker: Matthew Yglesias
insights.so_what: Relevant for anyone assessing NYC fiscal/municipal outlook: revenue capacity is not the binding constraint, capture by entrenched interests is — a durable structural headwind regardless of who is mayor.
insights.locator: too much of that revenue is captured by the city's various stationary bandits

themes.theme: Georgia's governor race as the key 2026 state battleground

insights.claim: The Georgia governor's race is the single most important winnable state race for Democrats because Georgia is large and populous, and because a Democratic governor paired with a conservative state legislature would enable moderate wins (Medicaid expansion, a small minimum-wage hike, reduced gerrymandering, and blocking any attempt to help Trump interfere with the 2028 election) without risk of a left-wing overreach.
insights.speaker: Matthew Yglesias
insights.so_what: Flags Georgia's governor race as a high-leverage political event to watch for state-policy outcomes like Medicaid expansion and election-administration risk.
insights.locator: I think the Georgia governor's race is very important

themes.theme: Why moderates don't run for office

insights.claim: Citing Andrew Hall's 2019 book 'Who Wants to Run?', holding political office — especially lower-ranking state legislature seats — is not an objectively attractive job for broadly capable people (low pay, heavy travel, public exposure), which skews the pool of people willing to run toward ideological extremes; the most direct fix is raising compensation and staff allowances for officeholders.
insights.speaker: Matthew Yglesias
insights.so_what: A structural explanation (and lever: pay/staffing reform) for candidate-quality problems that recruiting efforts alone won't fix.

themes.theme: Nuclear vs. solar: rejecting energy fandom

insights.claim: Yglesias disputes a claim from Ezra Klein's interview with Bill McKibben that California added the equivalent of 13 nuclear power plants of capacity via solar and batteries in three years, arguing battery capacity is not truly equivalent to nuclear baseload because a 1,100-megawatt nuclear plant delivers output much closer to genuine 24/7/365 availability than 1,100 megawatts of batteries.
insights.speaker: Matthew Yglesias
insights.so_what: A methodological caution for anyone using 'nuclear-equivalent' framing to evaluate battery/solar buildout claims — nameplate capacity comparisons overstate battery reliability versus baseload nuclear.
insights.locator: I also don't really think it's right to say that California's batteries are "equivalent"

insights.claim: Yglesias frames his own position not as pro-nuclear versus McKibben's pro-solar, but as favoring regulatory reform for both nuclear and utility-scale solar permitting, and criticizes mainstream environmental groups for being merely less hostile to solar than to nuclear or fossil fuels without actually backing the permitting reforms that would make utility-scale solar easier to build.
insights.speaker: Matthew Yglesias
insights.so_what: Identifies permitting reform — not technology choice — as the actual binding constraint on clean-energy buildout, a useful lens for evaluating energy-policy proposals and green-NGO positioning.
insights.quote: It's not a good idea to get too invested in energy technology fandoms
insights.locator: I really want to reject the framing

insights.claim: Texas is adding solar power capacity faster than California, which Yglesias uses to undercut the narrative that slow renewable buildout is primarily explained by fossil-fuel industry political influence over blue-state governments.
insights.speaker: Matthew Yglesias
insights.so_what: A concrete state-comparison data point challenging the 'fossil fuel lobbying blocks renewables' thesis — suggests permitting/regulatory environment, not lobbying dollars, best explains solar buildout speed.
insights.quote: they are adding solar power more quickly in Texas than they are in California
insights.locator: To the point I ended with above

insights.claim: Drawing on Julian Sanchez's roughly 16-year-old concept of 'epistemic closure' (originally applied to the American right), Yglesias argues a similar self-sealing dynamic has emerged on the climate/renewables left, where any pushback on green-NGO policy positions gets dismissed as fossil-fuel-funded 'shilling,' foreclosing genuine internal strategic debate.
insights.speaker: Matthew Yglesias
insights.so_what: A caution for investors and analysts consuming climate/energy advocacy commentary: watch for dismissal-by-motive-attribution as a sign that a movement's internal debate is not fully open.
insights.locator: it was more than 16 years ago that my friend Julian Sanchez coined the term
